salary, hourly, or commission, which one do you choose? Depends on the employee, depends on the staff member. I personally, never ever ever, my entire business, in the history of it, have ever gone with hourly. As a matter of fact, I think it's one of the worst options to possibly employ in business, because hourly employees are shit. Their mindsets are horrible. They constantly think with the 9-5 mentality of I'm gonna show up and then I'm gonna leave and I'm just gonna get the job done while I'm here. They don't work on a result-driven basis because they don't have any commission on their head and on top of that, let's be honest. They come out of a lower job pool than people who typically work in salary positions or in commission positions. You see, I personally have come from both of these roles and I've been in an hourly role before. I'll never forget my mindset when I was hourly. I was stealing from the place that I worked at at a grocery store at the time. I was just getting the job done. I was just a young kid doing whatever the fuck I wanted to because I was hourly. I was just there to get a paycheck. And my paycheck was low, so I didn't really care about the place and I didn't have any values that I hold as soon as I got a salary position, comparatively. I had a head of marketing position before I worked for Grant Cardone after I was done selling cell phones inside a Costco. And when I had that salary position, I was actually working longer hours for the individual, which is the same thing that I currently employ to my business. If you want essentially legal slave labor almost, where you get longer hours out of people than what you ever imagined possible, salary is the solution. You see, in a salary role, the individual cannot argue with you when you call them at 11 p.m. or 3 a.m. or you text them at those times and you say, hey, I need this done or I'm looking to do this or that. You see, they do have the ability to dictate the total amount of hours per se that they work in a day overall, but that doesn't dictate when or how they can work. It just is when you can submit requests, how you can submit requests and what timetables are allocated towards you allowing conversation. You can set aside time to do that or you can hit them up whenever they're on a salary. Now, you also get more hours out of people here, but you're also paying a flat rate. They're also responsible for taxes out of this. So, for example, my project manager, I pay $2,000 bi-weekly, so on the 5th and the 20th, I'm paying out $2,000 and $2,000. Pretty good deal. That being said, I get to work her any hours of the day, any hours of the week. I get to do her gone Saturday or Sunday. She came over yesterday, for example, for three hours. I didn't have to pay her to do that because she's on salary. It's essentially her showing up for a day of work almost. That being said, salary plus a little commission has been the greatest solution I've ever implemented in history. It gives people limitless potential in their minds that within a job, they have a limitless cap of how much revenue they can make. You see, when you give somebody commission, it actually does make them more money. And in most cases, people who are working in a job, they want more money and want to have more financial stability. So, give them more financial stability. Give them a taste, but you don't have to give them the whole piece of the pie. You see, as entrepreneurs, what most people don't understand is it looks like we're making all the money in the world, but in reality, most entrepreneurs are broke. I am not one of those entrepreneurs, neither were you. But that being said, most are broke because they spend all the money on expenses or on those travel trips they take, on all those things that they own, and all those materials. And while the story is we barely retain any revenue in most cases because most of us don't set aside profit. However, when you're in a salary position, you want to get that flat rate, plus you want to have some kind of incentive to work a little harder in order to get something else. That's how I have somebody who's in a salary position want to work around the clock and want to work on the weekends and more importantly, want to get results from my business. It's because they have a little bit of incentive for a little bit extra cash flow just because they're producing a little more than what they would if they only had a salary. Now, on the same perspective, on the same note, commission-only people are typically just sales and marketing managers. I've never put anybody else in a role inside of an organization where they're just commission-only. Sales and marketing managers, however, are people who are only commissioners. They're people who, in my business, get 20% of a sale when generated. And then on a recurring basis, they get 8%. You can set up your business differently. I personally love that number because sometimes I'll take that 20% and I'll make it 23% or 25% for when I want sales right away. If I say, hey, sales and marketing managers, if you guys generate sales today or tomorrow, instead of 20%, I'll pay 25% if you transact them by today or tomorrow. I look forward to you guys closing these deals. See, and then right away, I'm getting more people on the phones those days, magically, than I am any other day. It's interesting. It's almost as if they've built up their pipeline to the point where until they have those little incentives, that's when they choose to all of a sudden make a random appointment. All that being said, and it incentivizes people when it makes me happy because I'm negotiating 75% of something that I don't have. I'm negotiating 75% of zero. You see what I mean? So that 75% is worth more than me than 100% of nothing. So that being said, when you have somebody that's on commission just make a fair form, make it lucrative. I give my people 20%, some people say it's too high. I don't think so at all. We're having a business where we have tens of thousands of dollars made in a sale at one time. I want to pay thousands of dollars to a salesperson. Not so they can come to one, can place it, and then kind of go and hibernate until they need the next sale, but so they can actually make a large level of money. I actually condition my salesperson to rather than just go into a state of comfortability and hibernation when he gets a lump sum of money, to actually be more hungry. I've actually conditioned this individual to go out and spend typically that large level of money on materials or vacations or things on his family to reward him as a way to keep coming back to generating more and more revenue. The commission is an incredible game because it keeps people hungry. You just have to understand that if somebody gets a large level of commission out of what it is that you're doing with them, they may go and sleep on it. You have to be able to energize and motivate and re-emigrate these people to go back into business typically by encouraging them to go spend whatever it is that they just made. Most salespeople don't have a problem with doing this, but some of them will somehow condition themselves to have that safety-oriented mindset to be a saver and then not generate you shit until they run out of money again and now need to make another sale. Always, always, always educate anybody who's on commission. That way they understand that as they're on this commission scale, you are helping coach them to make sure that they're getting commissions when necessary and that you justify the coaching through that very means of saying, I need to help you get commissions. You help me? Salary or commission? I think it's sometimes with both. I've never paid a salesperson a commission. I'm sorry, I've never paid a salesperson a salary as a black-rate. I've made that mistake with that first salesperson if I mentioned. What an equity of my company. But, that being said, you need to understand that when you pay a salesperson on a flat rate, I paid that salesperson I mentioned who had an equity. The first person in my company I had to let go through that non-compete, a $500 rate every week, just to stand on their ass and not do anything. It was the absolute worst. It was straight robbery. That being said, you don't want to feel the same way. You want to always feel good about when you pay people and you want people to get a result. If they're on commission, they'll get you a result because they need to, otherwise they don't get a result. If they're on salary, give them a little bit of commission so they get you more results just by having that limitless potential. But, keep them comfortable in the salary position by giving those people a foundation as long as they're good staff and those are good employees. That being said, this has been salary, or hourly, or commission. It's not a faith or a 17-year-old faith. See you in the next video.
